hypothesis: The strength of the context representation in the PFC on trial N linearly predicts the neural discriminability of sensory stimuli in the relevant sensory cortex on trial N+1, supporting the Gain Modulation framework where frontal areas set the sensory gain for upcoming trials.
novelty_justification: The gain-modulation framework predicts trial-by-trial PFC-to-sensory gain transfer, but this specific cross-trial sequential regression (PFC confidence on trial N predicting sensory d-prime on trial N+1) in a context-switching mouse task has not been directly measured; the result is a quantitative test of a predicted but unmeasured effect.

regions.PFC: 7-727 units/subject (ILA, PL, ACA, ORB, FRP, DP)
regions.VIS: present in subjects with visual blocks
regions.AUD: present in subjects with auditory blocks

dataset_context.task: Visual/auditory context-switching, licking response (dynamic routing task)

supporting.title: Spatial working memory alters the efficacy of input to visual cortex
supporting.authors: Yaser Merrikhi, Kelsey L. Clark, E. Albarran et al.
supporting.claim: Prefrontal cortex modulates sensory signals in extrastriate visual cortex via direct projections from the frontal eye field; working memory-related FEF activity increases the gain of visual responses in V4 and MT without evoking spiking on its own, improving stimulus representation by neuronal populations.
supporting.excerpt: Although this signal alone does not evoke spiking responses in areas V4 and MT during memory, the gain of visual responses in these areas increases, and neuronal receptive fields expand and shift towards the remembered location, improving the stimulus representation by neuronal populations.

supporting.title: Prefrontal working memory signal controls phase-coded information within extrastriate cortex
supporting.authors: Mohsen Parto-Dezfouli, Isabel Vanegas, M. Zarei et al.
supporting.claim: Prefrontal working memory signals induce strong beta oscillations in V4 and phase-code visual information; pharmacological inactivation of frontal eye field PFC confirms that prefrontal signals are necessary for the working-memory-driven boost in phase coding of visual information in V4.
supporting.excerpt: WM induces strong beta oscillations in V4 and that the timing of action potentials relative to this oscillation reflects sensory information — i.e., a phase coding of visual information. Pharmacologically inactivating the Frontal Eye Field part of prefrontal cortex, we confirmed the necessity of prefrontal signals for the WM-driven boost in phase coding of visual information.

supporting.title: An integrative theory of prefrontal cortex function.
supporting.claim: Cognitive control stems from active maintenance of goal representations in PFC that provide bias signals to other brain structures, guiding the flow of activity along pathways that establish proper mappings between inputs, internal states, and outputs needed for a given task.

supporting.title: Frontoparietal Representations of Task Context Support the Flexible Control of Goal-Directed Cognition
supporting.authors: Michael L. Waskom, D. Kumaran, A. Gordon et al.
supporting.claim: Context is specifically and transiently represented in inferior frontal sulcus PFC during a sorting task; the strength of context representation correlates with the influence on task performance, supporting the prediction that PFC context strength influences downstream processing.
supporting.abstract: Cognitive control allows stimulus-response processing to be aligned with internal goals and is thus central to intelligent, purposeful behavior. Control is thought to depend in part on the active representation of task information in prefrontal cortex (PFC), which provides a source of contextual bias on perception, decision making, and action. In the present study, we investigated the organization, influences, and consequences of context representation as human subjects performed a cued sorting task that required them to flexibly judge the relationship between pairs of multivalent stimuli. Using a connectivity-based parcellation of PFC and multivariate decoding analyses, we determined that context is specifically and transiently represented in a region spanning the inferior frontal sulcus during context-dependent decision making. We also found strong evidence that decision context is represented within the intraparietal sulcus, an area previously shown to be functionally networked with the inferior frontal sulcus at rest and during task performance. Rule-guided allocation of attention to different stimulus dimensions produced discriminable patterns of activation in visual cortex, providing a signature of top-down bias over perception. Furthermore, demands on cognitive control arising from the task structure modulated context representation, which was found to be strongest after a shift in task rules. When context representation in frontoparietal areas increased in strength, as measured by the discriminability of high-dimensional activation patterns, the bias on attended stimulus features was enhanced. These results provide novel evidence that illuminates the mechanisms by which humans flexibly guide behavior in complex environments.

supporting.title: Task goals reorganize visual codes in human ventral temporal cortex via medial frontal modulation
supporting.authors: Robert Kim, T. G. Aquino, A. Mamelak et al.
supporting.claim: Visual representations in ventral temporal cortex are rapidly reconfigured by preceding task instructions; categorical representations are sharpened when goals are specified in advance, with dorsal anterior cingulate cortex carrying the reorganizing signal — showing PFC-to-sensory reorganization is goal-state-dependent.
supporting.excerpt: Identical visual stimuli evoked distinct responses in VTC depending on whether task instructions were provided or not, with categorical representations sharpened when goals were specified in advance.

supporting.title: Thalamic regulation of switching between cortical representations enables cognitive flexibility
supporting.claim: PFC responses reflect both individual cues and their meaning as task rules; the mediodorsal thalamus regulates switching between cortical representations, enabling cognitive flexibility in a task requiring mice to switch between visual and auditory rule sets — directly analogous to the context-switching task used here.
supporting.abstract: Interactions between the prefrontal cortex (PFC) and mediodorsal thalamus are critical for cognitive flexibility, yet the underlying computations are unknown. To investigate frontothalamic substrates of cognitive flexibility, we developed a behavioral task in which mice switched between different sets of learned cues that guided attention toward either visual or auditory targets. We found that PFC responses reflected both the individual cues and their meaning as task rules, indicating a hierarchical cue-to-rule transformation. Conversely, mediodorsal thalamus responses reflected the statistical regularity of cue presentation and were required for switching between such experimentally specified cueing contexts. A subset of these thalamic responses sustained context-relevant PFC representations, while another suppressed the context-irrelevant ones. Through modeling and experimental validation, we find that thalamic-mediated suppression may not only reduce PFC representational interference but could also preserve unused cortical traces for future use. Overall, our study provides a computational foundation for thalamic engagement in cognitive flexibility. Rikhye et al. recorded prefrontal and thalamic populations from mice performing attention selection across different contexts. By encoding context, the thalamus both enhances and suppresses prefrontal representations in a context-appropriate manner.

opposing.title: Baseline Shifts do not Predict Attentional Modulation of Target Processing During Feature-Based Visual Attention
opposing.authors: S. Fannon, C. Saron, G. Mangun
opposing.claim: Pre-stimulus baseline shifts in visual cortex attention-directing areas did not predict the magnitude of attentional modulation of target processing; using fMRI, baseline shifts in color-sensitive and motion-sensitive areas failed to predict target-evoked responses, challenging the hypothesis that prior frontal/parietal state linearly predicts subsequent sensory gain.
opposing.excerpt: Cues that direct selective attention to a spatial location have been observed to increase baseline neural activity in visual areas that represent a to-be-attended stimulus location. Analogous attention-related baseline shifts have also been observed in response to attention-directing cues for non-spatial stimulus features... baseline shifts in the relevant color- and motion-sensitive areas failed to predict the magnitude of attentional modulation of target-evoked responses.

opposing.title: On the Structure of Neuronal Population Activity under Fluctuations in Attentional State
opposing.authors: Alexander S. Ecker, George H. Denfield, M. Bethge
opposing.claim: Trial-to-trial fluctuations in attentional state induce response variability correlated across the neural population; this variability inflates noise correlations and can obscure the linear relationship between a gain-state proxy and sensory population responses, meaning cross-trial regression of SVM margin versus population d-prime may not recover the true gain relationship even if one exists.
opposing.excerpt: Both the focus and the strength of attention are likely to vary from one experimental trial to the next, thereby inducing response variability unknown to the experimenter... correlated activity arises only from trial-to-trial fluctuations of the attentional state, which are unknown to the experimenter.

opposing.title: Disinhibitory signaling enables flexible coding of top-down information
opposing.authors: T. G. Aquino, Robert Kim, Nuttida Rungratsameetaweemana
opposing.claim: Sensory neurons in low-level areas multiplex and dynamically combine bottom-up and top-down information via disinhibitory circuitry rather than simple gain scaling; this suggests a non-linear, gated mechanism (not a linear relationship) between frontal state and sensory discriminability.
opposing.excerpt: sensory neurons in low-level areas possess the remarkable ability to multiplex and dynamically combine both bottom-up and top-down information via disinhibitory signaling

contextual.title: Efficient coding theory of dynamic attentional modulation
contextual.claim: Attention dynamically allocates limited neural resources to task-relevant computations; sensory neurons are driven not only by stimuli but also by feedback signals from higher brain areas that adapt the sensory code to the goals of the organism, providing normative grounding for the gain modulation framework.
contextual.excerpt: sensory neurons are driven not only by external stimuli but also by feedback signals from higher brain areas. Attention is one particularly important internal signal whose presumed role is to modulate sensory representations such that they only encode information currently relevant to the organism at minimal cost.

contextual.title: Neural mechanisms of selective visual attention.
contextual.claim: Biased competition through top-down attention gates processing in extrastriate cortex — the foundational framework predicting that top-down context state selectively enhances relevant sensory representations.

contextual.title: Feature-based attention influences motion processing gain in macaque visual cortex
contextual.claim: Feature-based attention increases the gain of direction-selective neurons in visual cortical area MT without narrowing tuning curves, establishing that top-down signals can produce multiplicative gain changes on sensory populations.
contextual.excerpt: attention increases the gain of direction-selective neurons in visual cortical area MT without narrowing the direction-tuning curves

contextual.title: Prefrontal Computation as Active Inference
contextual.authors: Thomas Parr, R. Rikhye, Michael M. Halassa
contextual.claim: PFC performs active inference to maintain context representations via delay-period activity in rodent tasks requiring selection between concurrent visual and auditory targets — directly characterizing the computational role of PFC in context-switching tasks like the one used here.
contextual.abstract: Abstract The prefrontal cortex is vital for a range of cognitive processes, including working memory, attention, and decision-making. Notably, its absence impairs the performance of tasks requiring the maintenance of information through a delay period. In this paper, we formulate a rodent task—which requires maintenance of delay-period activity—as a Markov decision process and treat optimal task performance as an (active) inference problem. We simulate the behavior of a Bayes optimal mouse presented with 1 of 2 cues that instructs the selection of concurrent visual and auditory targets on a trial-by-trial basis. Formulating inference as message passing, we reproduce features of neuronal coupling within and between prefrontal regions engaged by this task. We focus on the micro-circuitry that underwrites delay-period activity and relate it to functional specialization within the prefrontal cortex in primates. Finally, we simulate the electrophysiological correlates of inference and demonstrate the consequences of lesions to each part of our in silico prefrontal cortex. In brief, this formulation suggests that recurrent excitatory connections—which support persistent neuronal activity—encode beliefs about transition probabilities over time. We argue that attentional modulation can be understood as the contextualization of sensory input by these persistent beliefs.

contextual.title: Context-dependent computation by recurrent dynamics in prefrontal cortex
contextual.authors: Valerio Mante, David Sussillo et al.
contextual.claim: Prefrontal recurrent dynamics implement context-dependent computation by flexibly selecting and integrating sensory inputs; the context signal is encoded as a subspace of PFC population activity from which motor outputs can be read out — providing mechanistic grounding for why PFC context strength would vary across trials.
contextual.abstract: Prefrontal cortex is thought to have a fundamental role in flexible, context-dependent behaviour, but the exact nature of the computations underlying this role remains largely unknown. In particular, individual prefrontal neurons often generate remarkably complex responses that defy deep understanding of their contribution to behaviour. Here we study prefrontal cortex activity in macaque monkeys trained to flexibly select and integrate noisy sensory inputs towards a choice. We find that the observed complexity and functional roles of single neurons are readily understood in the framework of a dynamical process unfolding at the level of the population. The population dynamics can be reproduced by a trained recurrent neural network, which suggests a previously unknown mechanism for selection and integration of task-relevant inputs. This mechanism indicates that selection and integration are two aspects of a single dynamical process unfolding within the same prefrontal circuits, and potentially provides a novel, general framework for understanding context-dependent computations.

contextual.title: Differential encoding of temporal context and expectation under representational drift across hierarchically connected areas
contextual.authors: David G. Wyrick, Nicholas Cain, Rylan S. Larsen
contextual.claim: Hierarchically connected visual and association areas differentially encode temporal context and expectation; a large fraction of variance in rodent visual responses is attributable to trial-history and contextual modulations — consistent with the premise that prior-trial state shapes next-trial sensory processing.
contextual.excerpt: a large fraction of variance of visual responses in rodents can be attributed to behavioral state and movements, trial-history, and salience

contextual.title: State-Dependent Regulation of Cortical Processing Speed via Gain Modulation
contextual.claim: Top-down gain modulation via cell-type-specific projections regulates cortical processing speed in a state-dependent manner; the computational framework classifies how perturbations to top-down projections affect stimulus processing in cortical circuits.
contextual.abstract: To thrive in dynamic environments, animals must be capable of rapidly and flexibly adapting behavioral responses to a changing context and internal state. Examples of behavioral flexibility include faster stimulus responses when attentive and slower responses when distracted. Contextual or state-dependent modulations may occur early in the cortical hierarchy and may be implemented via top-down projections from corticocortical or neuromodulatory pathways. However, the computational mechanisms mediating the effects of such projections are not known. Here, we introduce a theoretical framework to classify the effects of cell type-specific top-down perturbations on the information processing speed of cortical circuits. Our theory demonstrates that perturbation effects on stimulus processing can be predicted by intrinsic gain modulation, which controls the timescale of the circuit dynamics. Our theory leads to counterintuitive effects, such as improved performance with increased input variance. We tested the model predictions using large-scale electrophysiological recordings from the visual hierarchy in freely running mice, where we found that a decrease in single-cell intrinsic gain during locomotion led to an acceleration of visual processing. Our results establish a novel theory of cell type-specific perturbations, applicable to top-down modulation as well as optogenetic and pharmacological manipulations. Our theory links connectivity, dynamics, and information processing via gain modulation. SIGNIFICANCE STATEMENT To thrive in dynamic environments, animals adapt their behavior to changing circumstances and different internal states. Examples of behavioral flexibility include faster responses to sensory stimuli when attentive and slower responses when distracted. Previous work suggested that contextual modulations may be implemented via top-down inputs to sensory cortex coming from higher brain areas or neuromodulatory pathways. Here, we introduce a theory explaining how the speed at which sensory cortex processes incoming information is adjusted by changes in these top-down projections, which control the timescale of neural activity. We tested our model predictions in freely running mice, revealing that locomotion accelerates visual processing. Our theory is applicable to internal modulation as well as optogenetic and pharmacological manipulations and links circuit connectivity, dynamics, and information processing.

contextual.title: Near-random connections support top-down feature-based attentional modulations in early sensory cortex
contextual.claim: Top-down feedback from PFC to early sensory cortex can enhance gain of feature-selective neurons tuned to behaviorally relevant stimuli even via near-random connectivity; the global priming mechanism does not require precisely tuned anatomical connections, lowering the bar for PFC-to-sensory gain transfer.
contextual.excerpt: Top-down feedback from prefrontal cortex (PFC) can enhance the gain of feature selective neurons in early sensory areas that are tuned to behaviorally relevant stimuli

contextual.title: Different roles of response covariability and its attentional modulation in the sensory cortex and posterior parietal cortex
contextual.authors: Yong Jiang, Sheng He, Jiedong Zhang
contextual.claim: Attention significantly modulates response covariability in sensory cortex but not parietal cortex, improving neural encoding in sensory areas through covariability reduction; the stage of information processing determines how attention exerts its gain effects — sensory cortex is the locus of attention-driven discriminability change.
contextual.excerpt: attention significantly modulates response covariability in the sensory cortex and could improve the neural encoding at the sensory level

known: PFC projects top-down gain signals to sensory cortex that increase response gain and improve stimulus discriminability (Merrikhi et al., 2017, Nature Communications; Treue & Trujillo, 1999, Nature; Moran & Desimone, 1985, Science).

PFC represents and maintains context (task rule) information that biases downstream processing in a context-dependent manner (Miller & Cohen, 2001, Annual Review of Neuroscience; Waskom et al., 2014, Journal of Neuroscience; Mante et al., 2013, Nature).

Top-down feedback modulates sensory cortex response gain and covariability across multiple systems (attention, working memory) and species (macaque, human), providing the circuit substrate for PFC-to-sensory gain transfer (Desimone & Duncan, 1995; Jiang et al., 2023, PNAS).

In rodent context-switching tasks resembling the dynamic routing paradigm, PFC and mediodorsal thalamus cooperatively regulate switching between cortical representations; a large fraction of variance in visual responses is attributable to trial-history and contextual modulations (Rikhye et al., 2018; Wyrick et al., 2023).
unknown: Whether PFC context strength on trial N specifically and linearly predicts sensory discriminability on trial N+1 in a sequential, cross-trial manner has not been directly measured in any published study.

Whether the gain transfer mechanism is linear or threshold-gated: the plot from this experiment shows discrete clustering in sensory discriminability values rather than a continuous distribution, consistent with a gated or non-linear mechanism, but this has not been directly tested in the relevant circuit.

Whether trial-level SVM margin on PFC activity is a valid proxy for 'context representation strength' in the sense required by the gain modulation framework — the margin is sensitive to both context strength and irrelevant population variability sources (Ecker et al., 2015).

Whether the cross-trial lag relationship (N to N+1) is the appropriate timescale for PFC-driven sensory gain modulation, versus within-trial or block-level effects. No prior study has specifically tested lag-1 cross-trial prediction in this paradigm.
knowledge_map.how_this_sheds_light: The key decision node this experiment addresses is: should researchers invest in larger-scale sequential-trial analyses of PFC-to-sensory gain modulation, or is the linear gain-modulation hypothesis at the trial-by-trial timescale unlikely to hold in this task? A positive result (significant positive slope, prior 0.708 upheld) would constrain the unknown zone by providing the first direct quantitative measurement of lag-1 PFC-to-sensory gain transfer in a mouse context-switching task, promoting the contribution from contextual evidence to direct evidence. A negative result — which is what was found (slope = -0.003, p = 0.695) — rules out that strong PFC context representations linearly and directly predict next-trial sensory population d-prime at the N-to-N+1 lag, in this circuit, at this spatial and temporal scale. The contribution type is Constraint rather than Resolution because the null result does not close the gap: N=5 subjects provides unknown statistical power; the LDA-based d-prime metric discretizes into clusters (3 visible bands), making a linear model inappropriate; and the SVM margin proxy may not capture the full geometry of PFC context strength. What the experiment cannot resolve even with a positive result: whether the mechanism is causal (vs. correlational), whether it generalizes to larger N, and whether the linear model captures the dominant (possibly threshold) transfer function. The experiment successfully rules out a large, reliable, linear cross-trial relationship in this dataset.
knowledge_map.caveat_impact: No triage caveats were loaded for this experiment (caveats: []). However, the analysis and review together surface functional caveats: the discrete banding in sensory discriminability values indicates LDA d-prime is effectively an ordinal categorical variable rather than continuous — this violates the assumption of the linear mixed-effects model and means the experiment was underpowered to detect any relationship of the predicted form. Additionally, subject 713655 had only 7 PFC units, which is the minimum threshold; a reanalysis excluding this subject or requiring >= 20 PFC units would be a natural robustness check. These issues reduce the informativeness of the null result — the failure to reject may reflect methodological constraints more than biology.
